name: Dannon
why_now: Active Shaughnessy intro path and likely MODEX attendance signal.
primo_angle: Primo proof should land hardest here because both are high-volume food / beverage-style networks with plant throughput pressure.
notes: Mark Shaughnessy intro path to CSCO; closest analog to Primo.
fleet_profile: Contract carriers plus 3PL partners across fresh/chilled network
custom_hero: Fresh dairy has a 14-day shelf clock. Every hour your trailers wait in the yard is product life you never get back.

specific_pain_points.headline: Temperature-controlled yard dwell erodes shelf life
specific_pain_points.description: Danone moves fresh and chilled dairy products across 15+ plants. Reefer trailers sitting in yards without visibility into dock readiness means product shelf life burns before the case is even picked. At dairy margins, that dwell is measurable in shrink dollars.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: $3M-$5M in annual shrink exposure from excess yard dwell across the fresh network

specific_pain_points.headline: Plant-level yard protocols fragment cross-facility visibility
specific_pain_points.description: Each Danone production facility runs its own gate, dock, and spotter coordination. When the White Plains supply chain team wants a network view of trailer utilization or dock throughput, they are stitching together spreadsheets from 15 different local teams.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Zero real-time network visibility across the yard layer

specific_pain_points.headline: Carrier detention costs compound across the fresh network
specific_pain_points.description: Fresh dairy carriers operate on tight appointment windows. When dock contention pushes wait times past the threshold, detention charges hit every facility individually. No one is tracking the aggregate network-level carrier cost.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: $2M+ in annual detention and accessorial charges across the network

custom_problem_headline: Every Danone facility runs its own yard protocol. That is 15+ versions of the same bottleneck.

person_hero_overrides.Heiko Gerling: Heiko, 15 facilities. Four temperature profiles. One yard protocol. That is the standard Danone North America needs.
person_hero_overrides.Whitney Klemm: Whitney, the supply chain team sees network metrics. The yard is the layer where those metrics break.
person_hero_overrides.Jacqueline Beckman: Jacqueline, every carrier detention charge at a Danone plant is a procurement problem hiding in the yard.
person_hero_overrides.Annette Tolve: Annette, the demand plan is only as good as the dock execution that fulfills it. The yard is the gap.
person_hero_overrides.Jay Luikart: Jay, the Away From Home network runs on tight appointments. The yard is where appointment precision dies.

name: General Mills
primo_angle: Fresh supply chain leadership creates a window to discuss standardization and throughput across plants and DCs.
notes: High-fit food network; Primo story should travel cleanly.
custom_problem_headline: At General Mills scale, an extra 20 minutes per truck turn adds up to millions in hidden detention.

name: Frito-Lay
why_now: Public MODEX signal plus likely fit for multi-site execution standardization.
primo_angle: High-velocity snack network should respond to reduced gate friction, dock congestion, and more consistent trailer flow.
notes: High-throughput network and obvious yard complexity.
custom_problem_headline: Frito-Lay moves more snack trailers per day than most companies move per week. The yard should keep pace.

name: Diageo
why_now: Public MODEX signal plus likely fit for multi-site execution standardization.
primo_angle: Beverage flow is time-sensitive and network-heavy; YardFlow story should translate well from Primo.
notes: Beverage network should map well to the Primo narrative.
hq_location: New York, NY (NA HQ: Norwalk, CT)
fleet_profile: 3PL-heavy with contract carriers; mix of dry, temp-controlled, and high-value spirits loads
custom_hero: Diageo ages whiskey for 12 years, then loses 90 minutes at the dock door. The yard is your shortest aging process.

specific_pain_points.headline: High-value spirits shipments sit in untracked yard queues
specific_pain_points.description: A trailer of Johnnie Walker or Don Julio can hold $500K+ in product. When that trailer sits in a yard queue with no priority flagging or dock assignment logic, you are parking half a million dollars in the dark.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Working capital velocity: $500K+ per trailer sitting in yard without dock assignment

specific_pain_points.headline: Seasonal demand spikes overwhelm manual yard processes
specific_pain_points.description: Holiday season drives 3x throughput at Diageo distribution centers. The yard processes that work at baseline volume collapse under peak load. Gate queues back up, spotters are dispatched by phone calls, and dock turns drop below target.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: 15-20% throughput loss during peak season from yard bottleneck

specific_pain_points.headline: Multi-facility compliance and chain-of-custody gaps
specific_pain_points.description: Spirits distribution requires chain-of-custody documentation at every transfer point. Manual yard check-in processes create compliance gaps that the audit team catches months later.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Regulatory exposure across 30+ facilities

custom_problem_headline: Diageo ships premium product through yards that run the same way they did a decade ago.

name: Hormel Foods
primo_angle: Hormel runs 40+ facilities with a 700-tractor private fleet. A new CSCO inherits every yard protocol those facilities built independently.
notes: Strong fit for food manufacturing plus distribution motion.
fleet_profile: Private fleet of 700+ tractors plus contract carriers; heavy refrigerated
custom_hero: New supply chain leadership. 40+ facilities. Zero standardized yard protocol. The window to fix this is now.

specific_pain_points.headline: Leadership transition creates a yard standardization window
specific_pain_points.description: Hormel is appointing a new Chief Supply Chain Officer in March 2026. New supply chain leadership at a $12B manufacturer means every operational layer gets reviewed. The yard is the layer nobody has standardized yet.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: First 90-day leadership window to establish new operational standards

specific_pain_points.headline: Protein processing plants run 24/7 on live-animal schedules
specific_pain_points.description: Hormel's protein plants do not get to pause inbound. Live hog deliveries and fresh meat outbound operate on biological clocks, not shipping windows. When the yard backs up, the production line feels it in hours, not days.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Production downtime from yard backup: $200K+ per incident at a single facility

specific_pain_points.headline: Cold chain integrity breaks at the yard boundary
specific_pain_points.description: Refrigerated trailers maintain temp in transit, but yard dwell between gate and dock is where cold chain breaks happen. Across 40+ facilities with no standardized yard monitoring, Hormel has blind spots at every site.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: $4M+ annual exposure from temperature excursion events across the network

custom_problem_headline: Hormel runs cold chain at scale. The yard is the one link in that chain without real-time visibility.
custom_proof_quote: Primo Water operates 45 plants on the same production-and-distribution model. YardFlow cut their gate-to-dock time from 48 to 24 minutes.

custom_module_relevance.flowDRIVER: One gate process across 40+ protein, grocery, and cold-storage facilities.
custom_module_relevance.flowBOL: Captures inbound details before a live-animal delivery or reefer load even reaches the dock.
custom_module_relevance.flowSPOTTER: Sequences reefer and dry loads to the right dock type. 24/7 protein facilities need automated priority.
custom_module_relevance.flowTWIN: Network-wide dwell and throughput data the new CSCO needs from day one.

person_hero_overrides.Will Bonifant: Will, you are inheriting 40+ yards that have never been standardized. This is the window to set the network standard.
person_hero_overrides.Nicholas Schwartz: Nicholas, you cannot run a center-of-excellence planning model when every plant yard is a black box.
person_hero_overrides.Connor McKenney: Connor, the 24/7 protein line does not pause for dock congestion. The yard has to match the production clock.
person_hero_overrides.Anthony Nguyen: Anthony, carrier detention charges hit every Hormel facility independently. The yard is where those costs stack up.
person_hero_overrides.Tim Whitson: Tim, cold chain integrity depends on reefer dwell time in the yard. That is the one link without real-time data.

name: JM Smucker
why_now: New Chief Product Supply Officer role in February 2026.
primo_angle: Smucker runs coffee, snacks, and pet food through 25+ facilities. After the Hostess acquisition, three supply chains share one carrier base but zero unified yard protocol.
notes: Good balance of fit and logo value.
fleet_profile: Mix of contract carriers and 3PL; dry and temperature-sensitive loads
custom_hero: New product supply leadership at Smucker's. 25+ facilities still running local yard playbooks. This is the standardization trigger.

specific_pain_points.headline: New product supply leader inherits a fragmented yard network
specific_pain_points.description: JM Smucker created a new Chief Product Supply Officer role in February 2026. That leader inherits 25+ facilities across coffee, snacks, and pet food, each running different yard and dock processes. Standardization is a first-100-days initiative.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Leadership mandate window: first 100 days to define operational standards

specific_pain_points.headline: Three supply chains in one company, zero unified yard protocol
specific_pain_points.description: Post-acquisition of Hostess and the Jif/Folgers portfolio, Smucker operates coffee roasting, snack production, and pet food manufacturing. Each division brought its own yard practices. No one has unified them.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: 3 separate operational cultures running 25+ yards independently

specific_pain_points.headline: Carrier mix complexity across diversified product portfolio
specific_pain_points.description: Smucker ships coffee (temp-sensitive), pet food (heavy/palletized), and snacks (light/high-cube) through the same carrier base. The yard has to handle three different load profiles at every distribution center.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Dock assignment errors from mismatched load types: 8-12% of daily dock turns

custom_problem_headline: After the Hostess integration, Smucker runs more facilities than ever. The yard cannot scale on spreadsheets.
custom_proof_quote: When a beverage manufacturer integrated 45 plants onto one yard platform, they absorbed 15% more volume without adding dock staff.
custom_proof_attribution: Operations Director, Multi-Site Food & Beverage Manufacturer

custom_module_relevance.flowDRIVER: One gate process for coffee, snacks, and pet food. Three supply chains, one check-in standard.
custom_module_relevance.flowBOL: Captures load type at the gate so temp-sensitive coffee and heavy pet food pallets route correctly.
custom_module_relevance.flowSPOTTER: Sequences mixed load profiles - light high-cube snacks vs. heavy palletized pet food - to the right dock.
custom_module_relevance.flowTWIN: Network-wide visibility the new Chief Product Supply Officer needs to measure integration progress.

person_hero_overrides.Rob Ferguson: Rob, you inherited 25+ facilities from three different supply chains. The yard is the layer none of them standardized.
person_hero_overrides.Anbu Kuppusamy: Anbu, you manage $400M+ in transportation spend. Every dollar of detention that hides in the yard is spend you cannot optimize.
person_hero_overrides.Amadeo Wei: Amadeo, three acquisition supply chains, one procurement team. The yard is where those integration gaps surface daily.
person_hero_overrides.Jason Barr: Jason, carrier procurement gets harder when every facility runs a different yard. Standardize the yard, simplify the bid.
person_hero_overrides.Justin Middleton: Justin, warehousing ops end at the dock door. The yard between the gate and that door is where your throughput leaks.

name: The Home Depot
why_now: Official keynote tie gives unusually strong event signal.
primo_angle: Strongest public MODEX signal. Massive retail network makes execution variance expensive.
notes: Official keynote speaker Richard McPhail strengthens 2026 attendance signal.
fleet_profile: Heavy 3PL and contract carrier; flatbed, dry van, and LTL mix
custom_hero: Home Depot's CFO is keynoting MODEX 2026. Their DC network moves 10,000+ trailers a day. We should be in that conversation.
modex_speaker_title: CFO, The Home Depot (MODEX 2026 Keynote Speaker)

specific_pain_points.headline: 200+ facility yards with no standardized gate-to-dock protocol
specific_pain_points.description: The Home Depot operates the largest retail distribution network in home improvement, across RDCs, SDCs, DFCs, and flatbed DCs. Each facility type runs its own yard coordination. At 200+ sites, the variance is not a rounding error, it is a cost center.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: $50M+ in estimated annual yard-driven inefficiency across the DC network

specific_pain_points.headline: Pro and flatbed deliveries create yard complexity retail systems ignore
specific_pain_points.description: Home Depot is not just retail replenishment. Pro contractor delivery, flatbed staging, and will-call pickup create mixed-use yards that standard retail YMS cannot handle. Every flatbed DC is a different operational animal.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Pro segment growing 2x faster than DIY, compounding yard complexity

specific_pain_points.headline: Peak-season replenishment spikes overwhelm manual yard coordination at 80+ DCs
specific_pain_points.description: Home Depot's holiday and spring season surges push trailer volume 40-60% above baseline across every DC type. Manual yard coordination that barely works at average volume collapses during peaks. Overtime hours spike, dock utilization drops, and replenishment windows get missed.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: $15M+ in peak-season overtime and missed replenishment across the DC network

custom_problem_headline: 2,300 stores. 80+ DCs. Every trailer sitting idle at a dock is a missed replenishment window.

custom_module_relevance.flowDRIVER: Automates gate check-in across 200+ DCs. One process for RDCs, SDCs, and flatbed yards.
custom_module_relevance.flowBOL: Captures BOL data at the gate so dock assignment starts before the driver parks.
custom_module_relevance.flowSPOTTER: Sequences trailers to dock doors across mixed-use yards. Flatbed, dry van, LTL - handled.
custom_module_relevance.flowTWIN: Network-wide dwell visibility across every facility type. CFO-ready throughput data.

person_hero_overrides.John Deaton: John, 200+ facilities. Every DC type runs its own yard. The variance tax across that network is massive.
person_hero_overrides.John Drake: John, the transportation network you run moves 10,000 trailers a day. The yard is where that throughput stalls.
person_hero_overrides.Amit Kalra: Amit, RDCs, SDCs, flatbed DCs, fulfillment centers. Four facility types, four yard playbooks. One should work everywhere.
person_hero_overrides.Richard McPhail: Richard, you are keynoting MODEX 2026 on supply chain investment. The yard is the lowest-cost, highest-impact layer left.
person_hero_overrides.Erin Donnelly: Erin, supply chain development means standardizing the unstandardized. The yard is the last unstandardized layer.

name: Georgia Pacific
why_now: Public MODEX signal plus likely fit for multi-site execution standardization.
primo_angle: Industrial plant flow and trailer variability create real throughput drag across sites.
notes: Large plant/DC footprint and operational complexity.
fleet_profile: Private fleet plus contract carriers; heavy flatbed, dry van, and intermodal
custom_hero: Georgia Pacific runs 150+ manufacturing sites. Every mill yard is a different universe. Atlanta HQ is 10 miles from MODEX.

specific_pain_points.headline: Mill yards handle oversized and heavy loads that standard YMS cannot model
specific_pain_points.description: Georgia Pacific's paper and lumber mill yards are not retail DCs. Rolls of paper weigh tons, lumber has dimensional constraints, and flatbed staging areas operate on different rules than dock doors. Standard yard management software does not model this.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Mill-specific yard processes resist standardization without appropriate tooling

specific_pain_points.headline: Koch operational discipline demands measurable yard metrics
specific_pain_points.description: As a Koch Industries company, Georgia Pacific operates under a continuous improvement culture that measures everything. The yard is one of the last operational layers without standardized KPIs across the manufacturing network.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Koch management system requires quantified yard performance, currently unavailable

specific_pain_points.headline: Atlanta headquarters means MODEX is a home game
specific_pain_points.description: Georgia Pacific is headquartered in Atlanta, 10 miles from the Georgia World Congress Center where MODEX takes place. Supply chain leadership can attend without travel. Meeting friction is as low as it gets.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Zero travel barrier for GP supply chain leadership at MODEX 2026

custom_problem_headline: GP runs the highest-volume inbound yards in building products. Manual gate check-in is the bottleneck nobody measures.

name: H-E-B
why_now: Public MODEX signal plus likely fit for multi-site execution standardization.
primo_angle: Grocery distribution is brutally unforgiving; standardized site flow has obvious value.
notes: Grocery distribution makes the yard/flow story compelling.
fleet_profile: Largest private fleet in Texas grocery; 1,500+ tractors and trailers
custom_hero: H-E-B built the most admired grocery supply chain in America. The yard is the one layer they have not standardized yet.

specific_pain_points.headline: Texas-scale distribution means Texas-scale yard complexity
specific_pain_points.description: H-E-B serves 400+ stores from 50+ distribution and manufacturing facilities across Texas and Mexico. Their private fleet of 1,500+ vehicles moves 4,000+ trailers daily. The yard infrastructure connecting those assets has no unified operating protocol.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: $15M+ estimated annual yard inefficiency across the Texas network

specific_pain_points.headline: Fresh grocery margins demand sub-hour dock turns
specific_pain_points.description: In grocery, product life starts burning the moment it leaves cold storage. H-E-B's fresh and perishable categories require dock turns measured in minutes, not hours. Yard dwell is where that clock runs out.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Every 30 minutes of excess yard dwell = measurable shrink increase on perishables

specific_pain_points.headline: Own-brand manufacturing adds inbound complexity most grocers do not have
specific_pain_points.description: H-E-B manufactures many of its own products, from tortillas to ice cream. That means their distribution centers handle both internal manufacturing inbound and vendor inbound through the same yard. The scheduling complexity is unique.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Dual inbound streams (manufacturing + vendor) competing for the same dock doors

custom_problem_headline: H-E-B is famous for freshness. But fresh product loses shelf days in the yard before it ever hits the shelf.

name: Hyundai Motor America
why_now: Public MODEX signal plus likely fit for multi-site execution standardization.
primo_angle: Automotive has different physics than CPG, but flow variability still kills utilization and visibility.
notes: Cross-vertical proof target with manufacturing complexity.
fleet_profile: JIT carrier network with dedicated and contract carriers
custom_hero: Hyundai builds 1.3 million vehicles a year in the US. The yard is the gap between JIT delivery and the assembly line.

specific_pain_points.headline: JIT assembly depends on yard-level trailer sequencing that does not exist
specific_pain_points.description: Hyundai's Alabama assembly plant runs on just-in-time parts delivery. When inbound parts trailers arrive but cannot dock in sequence, the line either waits or runs rework. The problem is not the carrier. It is the yard between the gate and the dock.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Line stoppage from yard-related parts delay: $40K+/hour per assembly line

specific_pain_points.headline: EV production ramp adds battery logistics complexity to already-tight yards
specific_pain_points.description: Hyundai is investing $7.6B in a new EV and battery plant in Georgia. Battery module logistics require hazmat-compliant yard staging, separate from standard parts flow. The Georgia plant yard must handle two fundamentally different freight profiles.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: New Georgia EV plant requires purpose-built yard protocol from day one

specific_pain_points.headline: Multi-plant campus yard coordination runs on radio and phone calls
specific_pain_points.description: Hyundai's Montgomery complex includes assembly and engine manufacturing in adjacent facilities. Trailer moves between plants are coordinated manually. When parts are in the yard but the dock team does not know, the line sees a shortage that does not actually exist.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Phantom shortages from yard visibility gaps: 2-5 incidents per week

name: Honda
why_now: Public MODEX signal plus likely fit for multi-site execution standardization.
primo_angle: Large manufacturing campuses and logistics complexity make yard execution a real operational lever.
fleet_profile: JIT carrier network with dedicated cross-dock and milk run operations
custom_hero: Honda built its production system on eliminating waste. The yard between the gate and the line is where waste hides.

specific_pain_points.headline: Just-in-time assembly lines amplify every minute of yard delay
specific_pain_points.description: Honda's production lines operate on JIT principles where parts arrive in sequence within hours of installation. When an inbound parts trailer is delayed in the yard, the line does not wait. It runs out of sequence, creating rework or stoppage that costs $50K+ per hour.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Line stoppage from yard-related parts delay: $50K+/hour per assembly line

specific_pain_points.headline: Multi-plant campus yards have no centralized coordination
specific_pain_points.description: Honda's Marysville complex includes auto assembly, engine, and transmission plants sharing interconnected yards. Trailer moves between plants are coordinated by radio and phone calls. No single system tracks the campus-wide trailer position.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Zero campus-wide yard visibility across co-located manufacturing plants

specific_pain_points.headline: Carrier sequencing for line-side delivery starts in the yard
specific_pain_points.description: The Toyota Production System, which Honda adapted, assumes materials arrive in sequence. But the sequencing logic often stops at the gate. Inside the yard, trailers are parked wherever there is space. Sequence breaks. The line pays.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Parts sequencing errors traceable to yard parking: 3-5% of daily dock turns

name: John Deere
why_now: Public MODEX signal plus likely fit for multi-site execution standardization.
primo_angle: Complex industrial campuses and dealer/service expectations make standardization relevant.
notes: Strong strategic logo; worth cross-vertical test.
fleet_profile: Complex mix: flatbed for implements, dry van for parts, specialized for dealer delivery
custom_hero: Deere builds intelligent machines. The yards between their factories and their dealers still run on manual processes.

specific_pain_points.headline: Dealer delivery yards handle oversized equipment that breaks standard YMS models
specific_pain_points.description: John Deere ships combines, tractors, and implements that do not fit in a dock door. Dealer distribution yards need staging areas, flatbed coordination, and load-out sequencing that standard yard management systems were never designed for.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Specialized equipment staging errors cost 2-4 hours per misrouted load

specific_pain_points.headline: Aftermarket parts promise requires yard-level speed at 60+ depots
specific_pain_points.description: Deere's aftermarket parts business promises next-day availability for dealer service. That promise starts in the yard of every parts depot, where inbound replenishment and outbound dealer shipments compete for the same dock doors.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Parts availability SLA failures traceable to yard/dock contention at depot level

specific_pain_points.headline: Precision ag investment contrast: smart machines, manual yards
specific_pain_points.description: Deere invests billions in autonomous tractors and AI-powered precision agriculture. But the yards between their factories and their dealers still use paper logs and phone-dispatch spotters. The contrast is an easy boardroom story.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Internal narrative: 'We automate the field but not the yard'

custom_problem_headline: Deere builds machines that run autonomously. But every trailer still waits at the dock for a manual check-in.

custom_module_relevance.flowDRIVER: Handles oversized equipment staging. Combines and tractors do not fit in dock doors - flowDRIVER adapts.
custom_module_relevance.flowBOL: Captures load details for aftermarket parts, dealer delivery, and implements - different cargo, same process.
custom_module_relevance.flowSPOTTER: Sequences flatbed staging, parts inbound, and dealer outbound in shared yards. Priority-based, not first-come.
custom_module_relevance.flowTWIN: Yard-level visibility across 60+ depots, assembly plants, and foundries. One view for Moline.

person_hero_overrides.Cory Reed: Cory, Deere builds autonomous machines. The yards between the factory and the dealer are still the most manual process in the network.
person_hero_overrides.David Panjwani: David, 60+ facilities. 5,000 trailers a day. Global trade logistics that start with a manual gate check-in.
person_hero_overrides.Gia Duke: Gia, global supply management cannot run on local yard visibility. The gap is between what you see and what happens at the gate.
person_hero_overrides.Kevin Akers: Kevin, the operations strategy includes dealer-delivery speed. That speed starts in the yard, not on the road.
person_hero_overrides.Catherine Pham: Catherine, supply chain strategy is only as good as the execution layer. The yard is the execution layer nobody designed.

name: Kenco Logistics Services
why_now: Named session speaker indicates event involvement.
primo_angle: Less direct logo hunt, more partnership / ecosystem / referral opportunity.
notes: Useful ecosystem and referral play even if not first logo chase.
fleet_profile: Customer-dependent: manages yards for dozens of different shippers
custom_hero: Kenco manages 100+ warehouses for other companies. Every client yard runs differently. YardFlow is the protocol that standardizes them all.

specific_pain_points.headline: Contract logistics means managing 100 different yard protocols for 100 different clients
specific_pain_points.description: Kenco operates warehouses and DCs for dozens of different shippers, each with different carrier requirements, dock door preferences, and yard rules. The operational complexity is not the warehouse. It is the yard outside the warehouse, multiplied by every client contract.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Yard protocol fragmentation across 100+ client-operated facilities

specific_pain_points.headline: Innovation VP is speaking at MODEX, which means Kenco is buying, not browsing
specific_pain_points.description: Kristi Montgomery, Kenco's VP of Innovation, is a named session speaker at MODEX 2026. When the innovation leader is on stage, the operations team is walking the floor looking for technology to bring back.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Highest-quality MODEX signal: named speaker indicates active technology evaluation

specific_pain_points.headline: YardFlow as a multi-client yard protocol is a partnership play, not a single sale
specific_pain_points.description: If Kenco standardizes on YardFlow across their 100+ managed facilities, every one of their clients gets yard visibility. This is not a single logo sale. It is a distribution channel for YardFlow into dozens of enterprise supply chains.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Partnership potential: 1 contract = 100+ facility deployments

name: Barnes & Noble
why_now: Named session speaker indicates event involvement.
primo_angle: Named speaker gives strong attendance signal; retail replenishment pain can map well to yard flow.
notes: Annette Danek-Akey is an official MODEX speaker.
custom_hero: Barnes and Noble's supply chain leader is speaking at MODEX. Their DCs move 500+ trailers a day to restock 600 stores. We should meet.
modex_speaker_title: VP Supply Chain, Barnes & Noble (MODEX 2026 Speaker)

specific_pain_points.headline: Retail book replenishment operates on razor-thin timing windows
specific_pain_points.description: Barnes and Noble restocks 600+ stores from 8 distribution centers. Book releases are date-sensitive, and inventory replenishment windows are measured in days. When the yard backs up at a DC, store shelves go empty on release day.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: New release availability tied directly to DC-level dock turn performance

specific_pain_points.headline: E-commerce and store replenishment competing for the same dock doors
specific_pain_points.description: Barnes and Noble ships to stores and ships to customers from the same DCs. Store replenishment loads and e-commerce parcel picks compete for dock doors and yard space. Without yard-level prioritization, both channels suffer.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Dual-channel DC operations without yard-level channel prioritization

specific_pain_points.headline: Named MODEX speaker signals active supply chain investment evaluation
specific_pain_points.description: VP Supply Chain Annette Danek-Akey is a named session speaker at MODEX 2026. When supply chain leadership presents at the industry's largest event, they are signaling openness to new solutions and partnerships.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Direct meeting path: speaker attendance = high probability of floor presence

name: FedEx
why_now: Public MODEX signal plus likely fit for multi-site execution standardization.
primo_angle: Useful strategic logo, but more complex sell. Treat as opportunistic unless a warm path appears.
fleet_profile: Massive private fleet: 200K+ vehicles, plus contracted linehaul
custom_hero: FedEx moves 200,000 trailers a day across 5,000 facilities. Even a 1% yard efficiency gain is worth hundreds of millions.

specific_pain_points.headline: Ground-Express integration creates unprecedented yard complexity
specific_pain_points.description: FedEx is merging Ground and Express into a single operating network. That means thousands of facilities need to handle both parcel sort and freight cross-dock operations through the same yards. The integration is the largest yard coordination challenge in logistics history.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Network integration deadline: FY2027. Yard protocol unification is on the critical path.

specific_pain_points.headline: Scale makes even fractional yard improvements worth nine figures
specific_pain_points.description: At 200,000+ daily trailer moves, a 1% improvement in average yard turn time saves FedEx more than most companies spend on their entire logistics budget. The math is not theoretical. It is operational leverage at unprecedented scale.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: 1% yard efficiency gain = $200M+ annual impact at FedEx scale

specific_pain_points.headline: Strategic logo with long sales cycle requires opportunistic entry
specific_pain_points.description: FedEx is the ultimate strategic logo but the most complex sell. This is not a cold-call account. YardFlow needs a warm path, a pilot site, and patience. MODEX presence creates the opportunistic window.
specific_pain_points.kpiImpact: Treat as opportunistic: high upside, long cycle, need warm intro
